howItWorks.intro: This calculator estimates the amount of boneless meat you can expect from a deer, mule deer, or elk harvest. It uses industry-standard yield percentages that professional butchers rely on.
rules: Field dressed weight is approximately 78% of live weight (organs removed)
Hanging (skinned/headless) weight is about 75% of field dressed weight
Boneless meat yield is roughly 55-60% of hanging weight, or about 40-45% of live weight
howItWorks.outro: Actual yield varies based on the animal's condition, shot placement, field dressing skill, and how much you trim. These estimates assume a clean harvest with minimal meat loss. Freezer space is estimated at roughly 1 cubic foot per 30-35 lbs of packaged meat.

faq.question: How much meat do you get from a deer?
faq.answer: A typical whitetail deer with a live weight of 150-180 lbs yields about 60-80 lbs of boneless meat, roughly 40-45% of live weight. This varies with the deer's size, body condition, and how carefully it's butchered.

faq.question: What is the difference between live weight and field dressed weight?
faq.answer: Live weight is the deer's total weight before harvest. Field dressed weight is after the internal organs have been removed, which is typically about 78% of live weight. Most hunters weigh their deer field dressed since that's practical.

faq.question: How much freezer space do I need for a whole deer?
faq.answer: Plan on about 1 cubic foot of freezer space per 30-35 lbs of packaged meat. A typical whitetail yields 60-80 lbs of boneless meat, so you'll need roughly 2-3 cubic feet. A standard chest freezer (7 cubic feet) can hold two to three deer.

faq.question: What percentage of deer meat is ground venison?
faq.answer: About 35-45% of your boneless yield typically becomes ground venison, especially the tougher cuts like neck, shank, and trimmings. Many processors default to grinding anything that isn't a prime steak or roast cut.

faq.question: How much meat does an elk yield compared to a deer?
faq.answer: An elk yields significantly more meat than a whitetail deer. A bull elk at 700 lbs live weight can yield 280-315 lbs of boneless meat. That's roughly 4-5 times the yield of an average whitetail deer.

faq.question: How long can I keep venison in the freezer?
faq.answer: Properly wrapped and frozen venison stays good for 9-12 months in a standard freezer. Vacuum-sealed venison can last up to 2-3 years without significant quality loss. Ground venison should ideally be used within 6-8 months.

howWeCalculate.intro: This deer processing calculator uses established formulas and industry-standard data to provide accurate estimates.
steps: Enter your specific values into the calculator fields above
Our algorithm applies the relevant formulas using your inputs
Results are calculated instantly in your browser — nothing is sent to a server
Review the detailed breakdown to understand how each factor affects your result
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These calculations are estimates based on standard formulas. For critical decisions, always consult a qualified professional.

whenToUse.intro: This deer processing calculator is designed for anyone who needs quick, reliable estimates without complex spreadsheets or professional consultations.
scenarios: When you need a quick estimate before committing to a purchase or project
When comparing different options or scenarios side by side
When planning a budget and need to understand potential costs
When you want to verify a quote or estimate you've received from a professional
When teaching or learning about the concepts behind these calculations
